route_anchor_evidence.snippet: The cumulative mass (mg cm−2) was calculated based on this concentration. The flux (in mg cm−2 h−1) of the active drug – ibuprofen and its derivatives through the skin into acceptor fluid was determined as the slope of the plot of cumulative mass in the acceptor fluid versus time.
Table 1 shows the properties of ibuprofen and its L-valine ester derivatives.

evidence_items.snippet: The lidocaine or ibuprofen concentration of the obtained sample solution was measured using HPLC. The silicone membrane permeation rate of lidocaine or ibuprofen from IL preparations showed a steady-state flux, fluxss, after a short lag time period.

The lidocaine or ibuprofen concentration of the obtained sample solution was measured using HPLC. The silicone membrane permeation rate of lidocaine or ibuprofen from IL preparations showed a steady-state flux, fluxss, after a short lag time period.

route_anchor_evidence.locator: Theoretical and Observed Permeation Rates section
route_anchor_evidence.snippet: Theoretical and Observed Permeation Rates section
Figures 3a and 4a show the silicone membrane permeabilities of lidocaine and ibuprofen from IL preparations #1–9 with various fractions of lidocaine/ibuprofen.
Table 2 shows the molar fraction in IL, lidocaine, and ibuprofen at and after IL preparation.
